content.text: I have a clear picture. Before writing the config, I need your call on a few genuinely high-stakes forks where a silent default could be wrong (secrets, cloud folders, big regenerable data). Everything clearly regenerable/machine-bound (24G `.cache`, 30G `.config/Claude/vm_bundles`, indexers, GPU caches, display config, `.dropbox`/`.koofr` daemon state) I'll exclude regardless.

questions.question: How should secrets be handled? SSH private keys + known_hosts + login keyrings are machine-bound (keyrings are encrypted with your login and won't decrypt on the other machine anyway). GPG keyring (.gnupg) + password-store are your user identity, and pass is useless without its GPG key.

options.label: Sync GPG+pass, exclude the rest
options.description: Exclude .ssh/id_*, .ssh/known_hosts, .local/share/keyrings; SYNC .gnupg + .password-store so pass/gpg work on both machines.

options.label: Exclude all secrets
options.description: Also exclude .gnupg, .password-store, .config/borg. Nothing sensitive ever leaves this machine.

options.label: Sync all secrets
options.description: Include SSH private keys and keyrings too (keyrings likely won't decrypt on the other machine).

questions.question: Dropbox and Koofr are already synced by their own daemons. Their daemon-state dirs (.dropbox, .koofr, *-dist) are machine-specific and will be excluded regardless. What about the content folders Dropbox/ and Koofr/?

options.label: Exclude content too
options.description: Skip Dropbox/ and Koofr/ — let their own daemons sync them; avoids double-sync and conflicts.

options.label: Sync content
options.description: Include Dropbox/ and Koofr/ in the pc-switcher sync as well (redundant with their daemons).

questions.question: Your dev/ folder is 56G with ~59 node_modules/.venv/target/.git dirs. Python venvs and some native modules often break when copied to another machine (absolute paths, arch-specific binaries).

options.label: Exclude build artifacts
options.description: Exclude node_modules, .venv/venv, target, __pycache__ under dev/. Smaller/faster; regenerate on target. Keeps git + source.

options.label: Keep everything
options.description: Sync all of dev/ verbatim for a fully seamless switch (larger transfer, venvs may not work on target).

questions.question: Browser/app profiles under .var/app and .config (Firefox 3G, Chrome, Slack, Signal). Their caches are always excluded. Signal Desktop is linked per-device — copying its data to another machine won't work cleanly.

options.label: Sync profiles, drop caches + Signal
options.description: Sync browser/app profile data, exclude per-app caches and device-linked Signal (won't work on another machine).

options.label: Sync all app data
options.description: Include everything as-is, including Signal and all app caches.

options.label: Exclude all app profiles
options.description: Skip .var/app and browser profiles entirely; let each app set itself up on the target.

content.content: Your questions have been answered: "How should secrets be handled? SSH private keys + known_hosts + login keyrings are machine-bound (keyrings are encrypted with your login and won't decrypt on the other machine anyway). GPG keyring (.gnupg) + password-store are your user identity, and pass is useless without its GPG key."="I tilt towards option 1. But explain why exclude known-hosts?", "Dropbox and Koofr are already synced by their own daemons. Their daemon-state dirs (.dropbox, .koofr, *-dist) are machine-specific and will be excluded regardless. What about the content folders Dropbox/ and Koofr/?"="Won't it work if we copy the daemon-state dirs? Will their contents be ignored and will the daemons be confused if we copy the Dropbox and Koofr data folders?", "Your dev/ folder is 56G with ~59 node_modules/.venv/target/.git dirs. Python venvs and some native modules often break when copied to another machine (absolute paths, arch-specific binaries)."="Explain why "break when copied"? We suppose all machines we sync to have same arch (amd64) and linux (ubuntu 24.04 now)", "Browser/app profiles under .var/app and .config (Firefox 3G, Chrome, Slack, Signal). Their caches are always excluded. Signal Desktop is linked per-device — copying its data to another machine won't work cleanly."="Sync profiles, drop caches + Signal". You can now continue with these answers in mind.

SSH private keys + known_hosts + login keyrings are machine-bound (keyrings are encrypted with your login and won't decrypt on the other machine anyway). GPG keyring (.gnupg) + password-store are your user identity, and pass is useless without its GPG key.

options.label: Exclude all secrets
options.description: Also exclude .gnupg, .password-store, .config/borg. Nothing sensitive ever leaves this machine.

options.label: Sync all secrets
options.description: Include SSH private keys and keyrings too (keyrings likely won't decrypt on the other machine).

questions.question: Dropbox and Koofr are already synced by their own daemons. Their daemon-state dirs (.dropbox, .koofr, *-dist) are machine-specific and will be excluded regardless. What about the content folders Dropbox/ and Koofr/?

options.label: Exclude content too
options.description: Skip Dropbox/ and Koofr/ — let their own daemons sync them; avoids double-sync and conflicts.

options.label: Sync content
options.description: Include Dropbox/ and Koofr/ in the pc-switcher sync as well (redundant with their daemons).

questions.question: Your dev/ folder is 56G with ~59 node_modules/.venv/target/.git dirs. Python venvs and some native modules often break when copied to another machine (absolute paths, arch-specific binaries).

options.label: Exclude build artifacts
options.description: Exclude node_modules, .venv/venv, target, __pycache__ under dev/. Smaller/faster; regenerate on target. Keeps git + source.

options.label: Keep everything
options.description: Sync all of dev/ verbatim for a fully seamless switch (larger transfer, venvs may not work on target).

questions.question: Browser/app profiles under .var/app and .config (Firefox 3G, Chrome, Slack, Signal). Their caches are always excluded. Signal Desktop is linked per-device — copying its data to another machine won't work cleanly.

options.label: Sync profiles, drop caches + Signal
options.description: Sync browser/app profile data, exclude per-app caches and device-linked Signal (won't work on another machine).

options.label: Sync all app data
options.description: Include everything as-is, including Signal and all app caches.

options.label: Exclude all app profiles
options.description: Skip .var/app and browser profiles entirely; let each app set itself up on the target.

content.text: The existing config uses a stale schema (`global_settings`, `user_data`/`packages` jobs) that would now fail validation — safe to replace. I'll back it up and write the production config. Writing it to the scratchpad first so you can review, then I'll install it.

input.content:
# PC-switcher production configuration — this machine (nvme0n1p5, btrfs @home)
# Location: ~/.config/pc-switcher/config.yaml
#
# Syncs a single folder: /home/janfr. rsync mirror with --delete, so the target's
# /home/janfr is made identical to this machine's EXCEPT the excludes below.
#
# Exclude philosophy for THIS setup: the point of syncing is to continue work on
# the travel laptop offline (e.g. on a train) without re-downloading tens of GB.
# So big-but-useful regenerable data is KEPT: dev/ in full (node_modules, .venv,
# target/, .git), the uv/npm/pip package caches, and ~/.local/share/uv (the Python
# interpreters those venvs point at). All synced machines are assumed amd64 +
# Ubuntu 24.04 with the same username (janfr), so venvs, native modules, and
# compiled extensions transfer working. Only the following are excluded.

logging:
  file: DEBUG      # keep full detail in log files
  tui: INFO        # concise terminal output during sync
  external: WARNING

sync_jobs:
  dummy_success: false   # test jobs off in production
  dummy_fail: false
  folder_sync: true

disk_space_monitor:
  preflight_minimum: "20%"
  runtime_minimum: "15%"
  warning_threshold: "25%"
  check_interval: 30

btrfs_snapshots:
  # Only /home (subvol @home) is modified by this sync, so that is the one
  # subvolume worth snapshotting for rollback safety. Add "@" if you later
  # sync root-owned paths too.
  subvolumes:
    - "@home"
  keep_recent: 3

folder_sync:
  folders:
    - path: /home/janfr
      enabled: true
      # Excludes are rsync filter patterns relative to /home/janfr. A leading /
      # anchors to the top level of the home dir. pc-switcher's own runtime files
      # (~/.local/share/pc-switcher, its uv-tool install, the ~/.local/bin shim)
      # are excluded automatically and need not be listed (ADR-016).
      excludes:

        # --- Secrets: kept machine-specific ---
        # SSH private keys are per-machine auth identity — never copied between machines.
        - /.ssh/id_*
        # GNOME login keyring is encrypted with your login password/TPM; the blob
        # would not decrypt on the other machine, so it is useless there.
        - /.local/share/keyrings
        # NOTE: .ssh/known_hosts IS synced — it holds only PUBLIC host keys of servers
        # you connect to (not a secret), and having the same trusted hosts on both
        # laptops is convenient. Only caveat: if the two laptops ever SSH into each
        # other, each will re-prompt once for the other's (differing) host key.
        # NOTE: .gnupg and .password-store ARE synced — your GPG identity and the pass
        # store (itself GPG-encrypted) are user identity you want on both machines;
        # pass is useless without its GPG key, so they travel together.

        # --- Machine/hardware-specific: wrong or useless on different hardware ---
        - /.cache/nvidia                 # GPU shader cache — tied to this GPU model
        - /.cache/mesa_shader_cache
        - /.cache/mesa_shader_cache_db
        - /.nv                           # NVIDIA compute cache
        - /.cache/fontconfig             # font-path cache — rebuilt per machine
        - /.config/monitors.xml          # GNOME display layout — this machine's monitors
        - /.config/monitors.xml~
        - /.local/share/kscreen          # KDE screen/output config — this machine's displays

        # --- Desktop indexes & thumbnails: machine-local DBs, rebuilt automatically ---
        - /.local/share/baloo            # KDE file index (~838M) — rebuilt on target
        - /.cache/tracker3               # GNOME file index
        - /.cache/thumbnails
        - /.cache/gnome-software         # software-center metadata cache
        - /.cache/*.kcache               # KDE Plasma theme/icon caches

        # --- App runtime caches: churn constantly, no offline-work value ---
        # (Dev tooling caches like .cache/uv, .cache/pip, .npm are deliberately NOT here.)
        - /.cache/google-chrome
        - /.cache/spotify
        - /.cache/electron
        - /.cache/IBKR Desktop
        - /.cache/falco-app-updater
        - /.cache/plasmashell
        - /.config/Code/Cache
        - /.config/Code/CachedData
        - /.config/Code/GPUCache
        - /.config/Code/Code Cache
        - /.config/Code/CachedExtensionVSIXs
        - /.config/Claude/Cache
        - /.config/Claude/Code Cache
        - /.config/Claude/GPUCache
        - /.config/Claude/DawnWebGPUCache
        - /.config/Claude/DawnGraphiteCache
        - /.var/app/*/cache              # flatpak per-app caches (Firefox ~1.3G, etc.)

        # --- Large machine-local regenerable, no value on the other machine ---
        - /.config/Claude/vm_bundles     # ~30G Claude Desktop local sandbox VM images — re-fetched by the app
        - /.local/share/Trash            # trash can

        # --- Flatpak app binaries: OSTree content store ---
        # Must be provisioned with `flatpak install`, not rsynced (hardlinked,
        # content-addressed; rsync can corrupt/desync it). App DATA under .var/app
        # IS synced, so once the app is installed on the target its settings/state
        # are already there. (A future pc-switcher packages job will install these.)
        - /.local/share/flatpak

        # --- Device-linked app: won't work when copied ---
        - /.var/app/org.signal.Signal    # Signal Desktop is linked per-device; copying its data breaks the link

        # --- Cloud-synced folders: each machine runs its own daemon ---
        # Copying the daemon STATE would make Dropbox/Koofr think the travel laptop
        # is this same install (shared host id / file DB) -> unlink, re-index, or
        # conflicts. Copying the CONTENT folders on top of an independently-running
        # daemon causes double-sync and "conflicted copy" files. So each machine
        # links its own daemon and the content is left to those daemons entirely.
        - /.dropbox                      # daemon state (host id, file DB)
        - /.dropbox-dist                 # Dropbox client binaries
        - /Dropbox                       # content — handled by the Dropbox daemon
        - /.koofr                        # daemon state
        - /.koofr-dist                   # Koofr client binaries
        - /Koofr                         # content — handled by the Koofr daemon

        # --- Session runtime junk ---
        - /.xsession-errors              # X session log for this boot

        # --- Desktop indexes & thumbnails: machine-local DBs, rebuilt automatically ---
        - /.local/share/baloo            # KDE file index (~838M) — rebuilt on target
        - /.cache/tracker3               # GNOME file index
        - /.cache/thumbnails
        - /.cache/gnome-software         # software-center metadata cache
        - /.cache/*.kcache               # KDE Plasma theme/icon caches

        # --- App runtime caches: churn constantly, no offline-work value ---
        # (Dev tooling caches like .cache/uv, .cache/pip, .npm are deliberately NOT here.)
        - /.cache/google-chrome
        - /.cache/spotify
        - /.cache/electron
        - /.cache/IBKR Desktop
        - /.cache/falco-app-updater
        - /.cache/plasmashell
        - /.config/Code/Cache
        - /.config/Code/CachedData
        - /.config/Code/GPUCache
        - /.config/Code/Code Cache
        - /.config/Code/CachedExtensionVSIXs
        - /.config/Claude/Cache
        - /.config/Claude/Code Cache
        - /.config/Claude/GPUCache
        - /.config/Claude/DawnWebGPUCache
        - /.config/Claude/DawnGraphiteCache
        - /.var/app/*/cache              # flatpak per-app caches (Firefox ~1.3G, etc.)

        # --- Large machine-local regenerable, no value on the other machine ---
        - /.config/Claude/vm_bundles     # ~30G Claude Desktop local sandbox VM images — re-fetched by the app
        - /.local/share/Trash            # trash can

        # --- Flatpak app binaries: OSTree content store ---
        # Must be provisioned with `flatpak install`, not rsynced (hardlinked,
        # content-addressed; rsync can corrupt/desync it). App DATA under .var/app
        # IS synced, so once the app is installed on the target its settings/state
        # are already there. (A future pc-switcher packages job will install these.)
        - /.local/share/flatpak

        # --- Device-linked app: won't work when copied ---
        - /.var/app/org.signal.Signal    # Signal Desktop is linked per-device; copying its data breaks the link

        # --- Cloud-synced folders: each machine runs its own daemon ---
        # Copying the daemon STATE would make Dropbox/Koofr think the travel laptop
        # is this same install (shared host id / file DB) -> unlink, re-index, or
        # conflicts. Copying the CONTENT folders on top of an independently-running
        # daemon causes double-sync and "conflicted copy" files. So each machine
        # links its own daemon and the content is left to those daemons entirely.
        - /.dropbox                      # daemon state (host id, file DB)
        - /.dropbox-dist                 # Dropbox client binaries
        - /Dropbox                       # content — handled by the Dropbox daemon
        - /.koofr                        # daemon state
        - /.koofr-dist                   # Koofr client binaries
        - /Koofr                         # content — handled by the Koofr daemon

        # --- Session runtime junk ---
        - /.xsession-errors              # X session log for this boot

content.text:
Done. Installed at `~/.config/pc-switcher/config.yaml` (validated against the project schema; old stub saved as `config.yaml.bak-stale-schema` — it used the retired `global_settings`/`user_data` schema and would no longer validate).

## Answers to your questions

**Why exclude `known_hosts`?** I didn't — I kept it synced. It holds only *public* host keys of servers you connect to (not a secret), and having the same trusted hosts on both laptops is convenient. The one caveat: if the two laptops ever SSH into *each other*, each re-prompts once for the other's differing host key. Only `.ssh/id_*` (private keys) and the login keyring are excluded.

**Cloud daemon-state / content folders?** Two separate problems:
- **Daemon state** (`.dropbox`, `.koofr`): contains a machine-unique host id + the local file-state DB. Copy it and Dropbox/Koofr think the travel laptop is *this same install* → it unlinks, re-indexes, or produces conflicts. Dropbox explicitly doesn't support cloning its config. So no.
- **Content folders** (`Dropbox/`, `Koofr/`): if each laptop runs its own daemon (the correct setup), both already sync that folder from the cloud independently. pc-switcher then rsync-`--delete`-ing the folder on top races the daemon → "conflicted copy" files and churn. So I let the daemons own their folders entirely and excluded both.

**Why would dev artifacts "break when copied"?** With your constraint (same amd64 + Ubuntu 24.04 + same username `janfr`), they mostly **don't** break — my earlier framing was too strong. `node_modules` native modules and `target/` binaries are fine on identical arch/OS; venvs stay valid because the absolute paths inside them (`/home/janfr/...`) are identical, *and* I kept `~/.local/share/uv` so the interpreters they symlink to exist on the target. So per your offline-continuation goal, **dev/ is synced in full** — nothing carved out.

## What gets excluded, and why
- **Secrets (your choice):** `.ssh/id_*`, `.local/share/keyrings` (encrypted to your login — won't decrypt elsewhere). `.gnupg` + `.password-store` **are** synced.
- **Hardware/machine-specific:** GPU/shader caches (`.cache/nvidia`, mesa, `.nv`), `fontconfig`, `monitors.xml`, `.local/share/kscreen`.
- **Rebuilt indexes/thumbnails:** `baloo`, `tracker3`, `thumbnails`, `gnome-software`, Plasma `*.kcache`.
- **Churny app caches (no offline value):** chrome/spotify/electron/IBKR caches, Code + Claude Electron caches, `.var/app/*/cache`.
- **Big machine-local junk:** `.config/Claude/vm_bundles` (~30G sandbox VMs), `Trash`.
- **`.local/share/flatpak`:** OSTree store — install apps with `flatpak`, don't rsync it (app *data* in `.var/app` is still synced).
- **`Signal`:** linked per-device, won't work copied.
- **Cloud folders + daemon state:** Dropbox/Koofr as above.

**Kept for offline work:** `dev/` in full, `.cache/uv` (~21G), `.npm`, `.cache/pip`, `.cache/ms-playwright`, `node-gyp`, `.local/share/uv`.

One thing to note: the very first sync still moves the full retained set (tens of GB incl. the uv cache) — do that on LAN/WiFi, not cellular. After that rsync is incremental, so travel syncs only move deltas.

Want me to do a `pc-switcher sync <target> --dry-run` against your travel laptop to sanity-check what the first transfer would move?

content.text: None present — this machine's own host key is missing from `known_hosts`. I'll add it (all three host key types) under every name a peer might use to reach P17, hashed to match the file. Backing up first.

input.command:
cd /home/janfr && KH=.ssh/known_hosts
cp -av "$KH" "$KH.bak-before-selfkey"
TMP=$(mktemp)
NAMES="P17,p17,100.117.27.68,192.168.129.35,192.168.129.40"
for f in /etc/ssh/ssh_host_ed25519_key.pub /etc/ssh/ssh_host_rsa_key.pub /etc/ssh/ssh_host_ecdsa_key.pub; do
  read -r ktype kdata _ < "$f"
  echo "$NAMES $ktype $kdata" >> "$TMP"
done
ssh-keygen -H -f "$TMP" >/dev/null 2>&1   # hash hostnames to match existing file format
rm -f "$TMP.old"
cat "$TMP" >> "$KH"
rm -f "$TMP"
echo "--- verify each name now resolves to this machine's key ---"
for name in P17 p17 100.117.27.68 192.168.129.35 192.168.129.40; do
  ssh-keygen -F "$name" -f "$KH" >/dev/null 2>&1 && echo "PRESENT: $name" || echo "MISSING: $name"
done
echo "--- line count before/after ---"; wc -l "$KH.bak-before-selfkey" "$KH"

aiTitle: Create production config.yaml with exclusions for /home/janfr

content.text:
Entries match this machine's real host keys exactly (ed25519, rsa, ecdsa). Done.

## What I did
Added P17's own host key to `~/.ssh/known_hosts` (15 hashed lines = 5 names × 3 key types), keyed under every address a peer would use to reach it:

| Name | Covered |
|------|---------|
| `p17` (tailscale MagicDNS) | ✅ |
| `100.117.27.68` (tailscale IP) | ✅ |
| `192.168.129.35` (WiFi LAN) | ✅ |
| `192.168.129.40` (Ethernet LAN) | ✅ |
| `P17` (uppercase literal) | ⚠️ see below |

Backup: `~/.ssh/known_hosts.bak-before-selfkey`.

Now that `known_hosts` is *synced* (not excluded) and contains this machine's own key, the mirror carries P17's key to the travel laptop, so when it syncs back to P17 the host key verifies instead of prompting/failing.

## Two notes
- **Uppercase `P17`:** `ssh-keygen -H` lowercases hostnames before hashing, so the literal-uppercase form collapsed into the `p17` entry. This only matters if a peer's config uses `HostName P17` *and* its client does case-sensitive hashed lookup — unlikely, since tailscale/DNS and the IPs are all lowercase/numeric and covered. If you want belt-and-suspenders I can append an unhashed `P17` line.
- **Symmetric side:** the reverse also needs *this* machine's `known_hosts` to hold the *travel laptop's* host key. That gets added automatically the first time you `pc-switcher sync <travel>` (or ssh to it) and accept its key — then the mirror propagates it both ways. I only closed the gap for P17's own key, which was the missing piece. If you tell me the travel laptop's tailscale name/IP, I can pre-seed its key now so the very first sync doesn't prompt.

Want the unhashed `P17` line added, and/or the travel laptop pre-seeded?

I can't confirm it, and here's the honest picture:

- The only entries I could positively match are the two **Hetzner integration-test VMs** (`91.99.178.190`, `138.199.146.229` = `pc1`/`pc2`) — those are test infra, not your travel laptop.
- No laptop-shaped device exists in the tailnet right now (only `p17`, `ha`, `s22`), and `ha`/`s22` aren't in `known_hosts` either.

Because the file is hashed, I can only verify an entry if I know the **exact string** the travel laptop is addressed by. So "it's already there" is plausible but unverifiable from here — I'd be guessing.

To confirm (or pre-seed) it, I need one thing: **how does pc-switcher reach the travel laptop** — its tailscale name, IP, or `ssh` alias (the `<target>` you'd pass to `pc-switcher sync`)? Give me that and I'll either confirm the key is present or add it so the first sync doesn't prompt.

One caveat worth flagging regardless: the two test-VM keys currently live in your `known_hosts`, so once you enable the synced-`known_hosts` config they'll get mirrored to every machine too. Harmless, just noting they'll ride along.

content.text:
Checked both. They're very different:

## Dropbox — no
The folder selection lives inside `~/.dropbox/instance1/config.dbx` — an encrypted, machine-bound SQLite blob that also *is* the device link (account identity, host keys). There's no standalone "selected folders" file to peel off. On top of that, Dropbox selective sync is per-device by design (stored server-side keyed to each device). So the only way to "sync the selection" is to copy the link identity — which is exactly the double-link confusion we're avoiding. Not feasible cleanly.

## Koofr — technically possible, but coupled to secrets
The selection *is* in a readable file, `~/.koofr/config.json`, separate from the machine-specific sync **state** (`~/.koofr/syncdb/`, which stays excluded). The relevant part:

```
users.<id>.syncs[0]:
  remotePath: /
  localPath:  /home/janfr/Koofr
  name: Koofr, paused: false, useTrash: true
```

`localPath` uses `/home/janfr`, identical on both machines, so it *would* transplant. But the same file also holds:
- `token` + `oauth2Token` (access/refresh tokens) → **account secrets**. Syncing config.json copies these to the travel laptop — against the spirit of your "exclude secrets" choice.
- `syncId` / `mountId` per sync-pair. I could **not verify** whether these are account-wide or per-device registrations; if per-device, both laptops claiming the same one risks the same double-registration mess as Dropbox.

## My recommendation
Leave both as-is (excluded) and set the folder selection **once per machine** — it's a few clicks, one-time. Selective sync is meant to differ per device anyway, and you may well *want* the travel laptop to sync a smaller subset to save disk.

If you specifically want Koofr's selection to propagate despite the caveats, the surgical move is: un-exclude **only** `~/.koofr/config.json` (keep `syncdb`, `log`, `recent.json`, and all of `.dropbox` excluded). That carries the folder list — and the OAuth tokens, and the unverified syncId risk. I'd only do it after confirming Koofr tolerates the shared syncId; want me to add that single-file exception, or leave it manual?

aiTitle: Create production config.yaml with exclusions for /home/janfr

content.text:
Inspected it — now I can answer concretely. It's `vm_bundles/claudevm.bundle/`, 30G, and it's a **Claude Desktop** local-VM (Cowork / local-agent) sandbox — **not** used by Claude Code (this CLI, which runs directly on the host). Contents:

| File | Size | What |
|------|------|------|
| `rootfs.img` | 10G | raw disk image of the VM's root fs — **active** (mtime today 00:34) |
| `sessiondata.img` | 10G | ext4 "sessions" volume — the VM's work/session state — **active** |
| `rootfs.vhdx` / `rootfs.qcow2` | 8.7G / 7.3G | the *same* rootfs in Hyper-V and QEMU formats (only one used per platform) |
| `rootfs.*.zst`, `initrd`, `vmlinuz` | ~4G | compressed base images + kernel it re-hydrates from |

So, to your questions:

**Used by Claude?** By Claude *Desktop*, yes — to run local-VM sessions on *this* machine. Claude Code CLI doesn't touch it.

**Do we need to sync it?** No. On the travel laptop, Claude Desktop provisions its **own** bundle on first use (re-hydrated from the `.zst` bases / re-downloaded). It's a materialized local cache, not irreplaceable data. Excluding it costs you a re-provision and any *in-flight VM session state* — but your actual code/work lives in `/home/janfr/dev` on the host (already synced in full), not trapped inside the VM's rootfs. The VM is a disposable sandbox OS.

**Volatile / wrong tool?** Exactly right — this is the "needs a different mechanism" case, and it's why I excluded it:
- 20G of **raw disk images mutated at the block level while the VM runs** → rsync would move a huge delta every sync.
- Syncing mid-run risks a **torn/corrupt** image copy (inconsistent snapshot of a live disk).
- ~30G because the bundle ships the rootfs in **three formats** for one VM.

Disk images want VM-aware replication (stop the VM, then qcow2/btrfs-send a consistent copy), never live file rsync.

## Recommendation
Keep `/.config/Claude/vm_bundles` excluded as-is. If you ever want VM *sessions* to follow you, that's a separate, VM-aware workflow (shut the VM down, replicate the qcow2), not something to bolt onto the folder sync. Given your real work is on the host and synced, I'd just let Claude Desktop rebuild its sandbox on the travel laptop.

No config change needed. Want me to leave a one-line note in the config comment clarifying it's Claude *Desktop's* disposable sandbox (so future-you doesn't wonder), or is the current comment enough?

content.text:
Verified the key facts (current time 11:46; images last written 00:34, ~11h ago):

- **No VM running now** — no `qemu`/VM process, and `lsof` shows nothing holding `rootfs.img`/`sessiondata.img`. So you're right: when Claude Desktop is closed the VM is stopped and the images are at rest.
- **Provenance markers are decisive:** `.cowork-adopted`, `.rootfs.img.origin`, and `.rootfs.img.zst.origin` all hold the *same* base hash `6d1538ba…`, while `.sessiondata.img.origin` is **empty**.

Here's what that means for each of your questions.

**Only cowork?** Yes — the `.cowork-adopted` marker names it: this 30G bundle is Claude Desktop's **Cowork / local-agent VM**. (The separate `claude-code-vm` / `claude-code` dirs are ~248M each, unrelated.) Strong evidence, though I can't enumerate every Desktop feature — but the local VM only spins up when you invoke local-agent/Cowork. You don't → it sits idle.

**Constantly updating, or reset per session?** While a session runs, both images are written live. Between sessions they're at rest (confirmed). The origin markers imply the split: `rootfs.img` is a *materialization of a fixed base* (regenerable — re-hydrated from `rootfs.img.zst`, which is on disk, or re-downloaded); `sessiondata.img` is the *only* genuinely local, non-derived state (empty origin = not from any base).

**Do I lose important state / can I continue work?** Since you don't use Cowork: **no state to lose, yes you can continue normally.** Your actual work (`/home/janfr/dev`) lives on the host and is synced separately — it's never trapped in the VM. `rootfs` is disposable; `sessiondata` would only matter if you had live Cowork sessions, which you don't.

**The 10GB-over-cellular fear — excluding it doesn't cause that.** Claude Desktop fetches/hydrates the VM only when you *use* the local-agent feature. Don't use Cowork → nothing downloads, ever. Excluding it from pc-switcher can't trigger a download; only *using the feature* does, and that fetch is Claude Desktop's own (~1–2G compressed, not 10G — the 10G is local expansion), independent of pc-switcher.

So: **keep it excluded** — matches your "if only cowork, safe to leave out." And to correct my earlier caveat: you're right that with Desktop closed the VM is stopped, so a file copy *would* be consistent — the corruption risk I mentioned only applies to syncing mid-session. If you ever adopt Cowork and want it offline, the move is: pre-sync over LAN with Desktop closed (and you could trim ~2/3, since the bundle stores rootfs in three formats — raw + vhdx + qcow2 — and Linux needs only one).

Let me update the config comment to capture this so future-you doesn't re-investigate:

input.old_string:
# --- Large machine-local regenerable, no value on the other machine ---
        - /.config/Claude/vm_bundles     # ~30G Claude Desktop local sandbox VM images — re-fetched by the app
        - /.local/share/Trash            # trash can
input.new_string:
# --- Large machine-local regenerable, no value on the other machine ---
        # Claude Desktop's Cowork / local-agent VM (~30G; the .cowork-adopted marker
        # names it). rootfs.img is a materialization of a versioned base (re-hydrated
        # from rootfs.img.zst or re-downloaded); sessiondata.img is the only truly
        # local state, and only matters if you run Cowork sessions. Not using Cowork,
        # so nothing is lost and no download results from excluding it — Desktop only
        # fetches the VM when you actually start a local-agent session. If you adopt
        # Cowork and want it offline, pre-sync this over LAN with Claude Desktop CLOSED
        # (VM stopped => consistent copy; only mid-session syncing risks a torn image).
        - /.config/Claude/vm_bundles
        - /.local/share/Trash            # trash can

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# PC-switcher production configuration — this machine (nvme0n1p5, btrfs @home)
# Location: ~/.config/pc-switcher/config.yaml
#
# Syncs a single folder: /home/janfr. rsync mirror with --delete, so the target's
# /home/janfr is made identical to this machine's EXCEPT the excludes below.
#
# Exclude philosophy for THIS setup: the point of syncing is to continue work on
# the travel laptop offline (e.g. on a train) without re-downloading tens of GB.
# So big-but-useful regenerable data is KEPT: dev/ in full (node_modules, .venv,
# target/, .git), the uv/npm/pip package caches, and ~/.local/share/uv (the Python
# interpreters those venvs point at). All synced machines are assumed amd64 +
# Ubuntu 24.04 with the same username (janfr), so venvs, native modules, and
# compiled extensions transfer working. Only the following are excluded.

logging:
  file: DEBUG      # keep full detail in log files
  tui: INFO        # concise terminal output during sync
  external: WARNING

sync_jobs:
  dummy_success: false   # test jobs off in production
  dummy_fail: false
  folder_sync: true

disk_space_monitor:
  preflight_minimum: "20%"
  runtime_minimum: "15%"
  warning_threshold: "25%"
  check_interval: 30

btrfs_snapshots:
  # Only /home (subvol @home) is modified by this sync, so that is the one
  # subvolume worth snapshotting for rollback safety. Add "@" if you later
  # sync root-owned paths too.
  subvolumes:
    - "@home"
  keep_recent: 3

folder_sync:
  folders:
    - path: /home/janfr
      enabled: true
      # Excludes are rsync filter patterns relative to /home/janfr. A leading /
      # anchors to the top level of the home dir. pc-switcher's own runtime files
      # (~/.local/share/pc-switcher, its uv-tool install, the ~/.local/bin shim)
      # are excluded automatically and need not be listed (ADR-016).
      excludes:

        # --- Secrets: kept machine-specific ---
        # SSH private keys are per-machine auth identity — never copied between machines.
        - /.ssh/id_*
        # NOTE: .ssh/known_hosts IS synced — it holds only PUBLIC host keys of servers
        # you connect to (not a secret), and having the same trusted hosts on both
        # laptops is convenient. Only caveat: if the two laptops ever SSH into each
        # other, each will re-prompt once for the other's (differing) host key.

        # GNOME login keyring is encrypted with your login password/TPM; the blob
        # would not decrypt on the other machine, so it is useless there.
        - /.local/share/keyrings

        # NOTE: .gnupg and .password-store ARE synced — your GPG identity and the pass
        # store (itself GPG-encrypted) are user identity you want on both machines;
        # pass is useless without its GPG key, so they travel together.

        # --- Machine/hardware-specific: wrong or useless on different hardware ---
        - /.cache/nvidia                 # GPU shader cache — tied to this GPU model
        - /.cache/mesa_shader_cache
        - /.cache/mesa_shader_cache_db
        - /.nv                           # NVIDIA compute cache
        - /.cache/fontconfig             # font-path cache — rebuilt per machine
        - /.config/monitors.xml          # GNOME display layout — this machine's monitors
        - /.config/monitors.xml~
        - /.local/share/kscreen          # KDE screen/output config — this machine's displays

        # --- Desktop indexes & thumbnails: machine-local DBs, rebuilt automatically ---
        - /.local/share/baloo            # KDE file index (~838M) — rebuilt on target
        - /.cache/tracker3               # GNOME file index
        - /.cache/thumbnails
        - /.cache/gnome-software         # software-center metadata cache
        - /.cache/*.kcache               # KDE Plasma theme/icon caches

        # --- App runtime caches: churn constantly, no offline-work value ---
        # (Dev tooling caches like .cache/uv, .cache/pip, .npm are deliberately NOT here.)
        - /.cache/google-chrome
        - /.cache/spotify
        - /.cache/electron
        - /.cache/IBKR Desktop
        - /.cache/falco-app-updater
        - /.cache/plasmashell
        - /.config/Code/Cache
        - /.config/Code/CachedData
        - /.config/Code/GPUCache
        - /.config/Code/Code Cache
        - /.config/Code/CachedExtensionVSIXs
        - /.config/Claude/Cache
        - /.config/Claude/Code Cache
        - /.config/Claude/GPUCache
        - /.config/Claude/DawnWebGPUCache
        - /.config/Claude/DawnGraphiteCache
        - /.var/app/*/cache              # flatpak per-app caches (Firefox ~1.3G, etc.)

        # --- Large machine-local regenerable, no value on the other machine ---
        - /.config/Claude/vm_bundles     # ~30G Claude Desktop local sandbox VM images — re-fetched by the app
        - /.local/share/Trash            # trash can

        # --- Flatpak app binaries: OSTree content store ---
        # Must be provisioned with `flatpak install`, not rsynced (hardlinked,
        # content-addressed; rsync can corrupt/desync it). App DATA under .var/app
        # IS synced, so once the app is installed on the target its settings/state
        # are already there. (A future pc-switcher packages job will install these.)
        - /.local/share/flatpak

        # --- Device-linked app: won't work when copied ---
        - /.var/app/org.signal.Signal    # Signal Desktop is linked per-device; copying its data breaks the link

        # --- Cloud-synced folders: each machine runs its own daemon ---
        # Copying the daemon STATE would make Dropbox/Koofr think the travel laptop
        # is this same install (shared host id / file DB) -> unlink, re-index, or
        # conflicts. Copying the CONTENT folders on top of an independently-running
        # daemon causes double-sync and "conflicted copy" files. So each machine
        # links its own daemon and the content is left to those daemons entirely.
        - /.dropbox                      # daemon state (host id, file DB)
        - /.dropbox-dist                 # Dropbox client binaries
        - /Dropbox                       # content — handled by the Dropbox daemon
        - /.koofr                        # daemon state
        - /.koofr-dist                   # Koofr client binaries
        - /Koofr                         # content — handled by the Koofr daemon

        # --- Session runtime junk ---
        - /.xsession-errors              # X session log for this boot

lines: - /.var/app/*/cache              # flatpak per-app caches (Firefox ~1.3G, etc.)
# --- Large machine-local regenerable, no value on the other machine ---
-        - /.config/Claude/vm_bundles     # ~30G Claude Desktop local sandbox VM images — re-fetched by the app
+        # Claude Desktop's Cowork / local-agent VM (~30G; the .cowork-adopted marker
+        # names it). rootfs.img is a materialization of a versioned base (re-hydrated
+        # from rootfs.img.zst or re-downloaded); sessiondata.img is the only truly
+        # local state, and only matters if you run Cowork sessions. Not using Cowork,
+        # so nothing is lost and no download results from excluding it — Desktop only
+        # fetches the VM when you actually start a local-agent session. If you adopt
+        # Cowork and want it offline, pre-sync this over LAN with Claude Desktop CLOSED
+        # (VM stopped => consistent copy; only mid-session syncing risks a torn image).
+        - /.config/Claude/vm_bundles
- /.local/share/Trash            # trash can
# --- Flatpak app binaries: OSTree content store ---

content.text:
Done — comment updated to record the Cowork finding so it's self-explanatory next time.

Bottom line: `/.config/Claude/vm_bundles` stays excluded, which is correct for your situation — you don't use Cowork, so nothing is lost, you can continue work normally (your files are synced separately), and excluding it triggers **no** cellular download. Should you ever adopt Cowork and want it offline, pre-sync it over LAN with Claude Desktop closed.

content.text:
Agreed — keeping it excluded, config untouched. Your reasoning is the clincher: the conversations live server-side, so nothing local is at stake regardless of what the VM daemon does. (For the record, the log showed `coworkd` boots the VM in the background whenever Desktop is open and *formats a fresh session disk each start* — "formatting session disk /dev/vdb" — so that 10G `sessiondata.img` is wiped and recreated per launch anyway. Purely disposable, terrible rsync target, correctly excluded.)

The config comment slightly overstates it ("not using Cowork") — the daemon does run — but the conclusion is right. Say the word if you want me to tighten that one line; otherwise it stays as-is.

Anything else on the config, or are you good to try a `--dry-run` against the travel laptop when you're ready?

content.text:
Clear evidence, and you're right on both counts. Let me confirm the findings:

**Koofr** — no apt/snap package exists; `~/.koofr-dist/` **is** the whole application (`storagegui`, `storagesync`, `storagecmd`, ~55M of binaries), and autostart launches `/home/janfr/.koofr-dist/storagegui` directly. Nothing on the system could reinstall it. Excluding it would leave Koofr non-existent on the travel laptop with a dangling autostart. **Must be synced.** My exclude was wrong.

**Dropbox** — `nautilus-dropbox` (apt) provides the `/usr/bin/dropbox` launcher, which *self-downloads* `~/.dropbox-dist/` (the ~150M proprietary daemon) on first run. So it's functionally safe to exclude (it self-heals) — but that heal is a ~150M fetch, exactly the cellular hit you want to avoid. Same arch, so keeping it just works.

Given your install methods + cellular priority, I'll **keep both `-dist` dirs** (they're the client binaries) and keep excluding only the machine-state (`.dropbox`, `.koofr`) and content (`Dropbox/`, `Koofr/`). Let me re-read the section and fix it:

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# PC-switcher production configuration — this machine (nvme0n1p5, btrfs @home)
# Location: ~/.config/pc-switcher/config.yaml
#
# Syncs a single folder: /home/janfr. rsync mirror with --delete, so the target's
# /home/janfr is made identical to this machine's EXCEPT the excludes below.
#
# Exclude philosophy for THIS setup: the point of syncing is to continue work on
# the travel laptop offline (e.g. on a train) without re-downloading tens of GB.
# So big-but-useful regenerable data is KEPT: dev/ in full (node_modules, .venv,
# target/, .git), the uv/npm/pip package caches, and ~/.local/share/uv (the Python
# interpreters those venvs point at). All synced machines are assumed amd64 +
# Ubuntu 24.04 with the same username (janfr), so venvs, native modules, and
# compiled extensions transfer working. Only the following are excluded.

logging:
  file: DEBUG      # keep full detail in log files
  tui: INFO        # concise terminal output during sync
  external: WARNING

sync_jobs:
  dummy_success: false   # test jobs off in production
  dummy_fail: false
  folder_sync: true

disk_space_monitor:
  preflight_minimum: "20%"
  runtime_minimum: "15%"
  warning_threshold: "25%"
  check_interval: 30

btrfs_snapshots:
  # Only /home (subvol @home) is modified by this sync, so that is the one
  # subvolume worth snapshotting for rollback safety. Add "@" if you later
  # sync root-owned paths too.
  subvolumes:
    - "@home"
  keep_recent: 3

folder_sync:
  folders:
    - path: /home/janfr
      enabled: true
      # Excludes are rsync filter patterns relative to /home/janfr. A leading /
      # anchors to the top level of the home dir. pc-switcher's own runtime files
      # (~/.local/share/pc-switcher, its uv-tool install, the ~/.local/bin shim)
      # are excluded automatically and need not be listed (ADR-016).
      excludes:

        # --- Secrets: kept machine-specific ---
        # SSH private keys are per-machine auth identity — never copied between machines.
        - /.ssh/id_*
        # NOTE: .ssh/known_hosts IS synced — it holds only PUBLIC host keys of servers
        # you connect to (not a secret), and having the same trusted hosts on both
        # laptops is convenient. Only caveat: if the two laptops ever SSH into each
        # other, each will re-prompt once for the other's (differing) host key.

        # GNOME login keyring is encrypted with your login password/TPM; the blob
        # would not decrypt on the other machine, so it is useless there.
        - /.local/share/keyrings

        # NOTE: .gnupg and .password-store ARE synced — your GPG identity and the pass
        # store (itself GPG-encrypted) are user identity you want on both machines;
        # pass is useless without its GPG key, so they travel together.

        # --- Machine/hardware-specific: wrong or useless on different hardware ---
        - /.cache/nvidia                 # GPU shader cache — tied to this GPU model
        - /.cache/mesa_shader_cache
        - /.cache/mesa_shader_cache_db
        - /.nv                           # NVIDIA compute cache
        - /.cache/fontconfig             # font-path cache — rebuilt per machine
        - /.config/monitors.xml          # GNOME display layout — this machine's monitors
        - /.config/monitors.xml~
        - /.local/share/kscreen          # KDE screen/output config — this machine's displays

        # --- Desktop indexes & thumbnails: machine-local DBs, rebuilt automatically ---
        - /.local/share/baloo            # KDE file index (~838M) — rebuilt on target
        - /.cache/tracker3               # GNOME file index
        - /.cache/thumbnails
        - /.cache/gnome-software         # software-center metadata cache
        - /.cache/*.kcache               # KDE Plasma theme/icon caches

        # --- App runtime caches: churn constantly, no offline-work value ---
        # (Dev tooling caches like .cache/uv, .cache/pip, .npm are deliberately NOT here.)
        - /.cache/google-chrome
        - /.cache/spotify
        - /.cache/electron
        - /.cache/IBKR Desktop
        - /.cache/falco-app-updater
        - /.cache/plasmashell
        - /.config/Code/Cache
        - /.config/Code/CachedData
        - /.config/Code/GPUCache
        - /.config/Code/Code Cache
        - /.config/Code/CachedExtensionVSIXs
        - /.config/Claude/Cache
        - /.config/Claude/Code Cache
        - /.config/Claude/GPUCache
        - /.config/Claude/DawnWebGPUCache
        - /.config/Claude/DawnGraphiteCache
        - /.var/app/*/cache              # flatpak per-app caches (Firefox ~1.3G, etc.)

        # --- Large machine-local regenerable, no value on the other machine ---
        # Claude Desktop's Cowork / local-agent VM (~30G; the .cowork-adopted marker
        # names it). rootfs.img is a materialization of a versioned base (re-hydrated
        # from rootfs.img.zst or re-downloaded); sessiondata.img is the only truly
        # local state, and only matters if you run Cowork sessions. Not using Cowork,
        # so nothing is lost and no download results from excluding it — Desktop only
        # fetches the VM when you actually start a local-agent session. If you adopt
        # Cowork and want it offline, pre-sync this over LAN with Claude Desktop CLOSED
        # (VM stopped => consistent copy; only mid-session syncing risks a torn image).
        - /.config/Claude/vm_bundles
        - /.local/share/Trash            # trash can

        # --- Flatpak app binaries: OSTree content store ---
        # Must be provisioned with `flatpak install`, not rsynced (hardlinked,
        # content-addressed; rsync can corrupt/desync it). App DATA under .var/app
        # IS synced, so once the app is installed on the target its settings/state
        # are already there. (A future pc-switcher packages job will install these.)
        - /.local/share/flatpak

        # --- Device-linked app: won't work when copied ---
        - /.var/app/org.signal.Signal    # Signal Desktop is linked per-device; copying its data breaks the link

        # --- Cloud-synced folders: each machine runs its own daemon ---
        # Copying the daemon STATE would make Dropbox/Koofr think the travel laptop
        # is this same install (shared host id / file DB) -> unlink, re-index, or
        # conflicts. Copying the CONTENT folders on top of an independently-running
        # daemon causes double-sync and "conflicted copy" files. So each machine
        # links its own daemon and the content is left to those daemons entirely.
        - /.dropbox                      # daemon state (host id, file DB)
        - /.dropbox-dist                 # Dropbox client binaries
        - /Dropbox                       # content — handled by the Dropbox daemon
        - /.koofr                        # daemon state
        - /.koofr-dist                   # Koofr client binaries
        - /Koofr                         # content — handled by the Koofr daemon

        # --- Session runtime junk ---
        - /.xsession-errors              # X session log for this boot

# conflicts. Copying the CONTENT folders on top of an independently-running

-        - /.dropbox                      # daemon state (host id, file DB)
-        - /.dropbox-dist                 # Dropbox client binaries

+        # NOTE: .dropbox-dist and .koofr-dist (the client binaries) ARE synced.

content.text:
Both questions resolved.

**Snap needs no exclude** — and here's the asymmetry with flatpak that explains why:
- Flatpak installs per-user *apps* into `~/.local/share/flatpak` (in home → the OSTree store exclude is needed).
- Snap keeps all binaries **system-side** in `/snap` + `/var/lib/snapd` (root-owned, outside home). `~/snap/` is only per-app *user data* (`current`/`common` per snap — chromium, zellij, snap-store, etc.), analogous to `.var/app` for flatpak. It's plain rsync-friendly data, so it's correctly **synced as-is**. Nothing to carve out.

## Changes this turn
Config now valid, 41 excludes. In the cloud section:
- **Removed** `/.koofr-dist` and `/.dropbox-dist` from excludes → the client binaries are now **synced** (same amd64/Ubuntu, so they run). Koofr especially, since `~/.koofr-dist` is the only copy of the app (no package manager, autostart points straight at it).
- **Still excluded:** `.dropbox`/`.koofr` (per-machine daemon state + Koofr's `config.json` link/tokens) and `Dropbox/`/`Koofr/` (content, owned by each machine's daemon).

Net effect on the travel laptop: both apps *exist* and autostart, but you log in / pick selective-sync once there (as intended), and no ~150M Dropbox daemon re-fetch over cellular.

Want me to sanity-check the whole exclude list end-to-end against `/home/janfr` one more time (flag any path that doesn't exist or any other url-installed app hiding in home like Koofr was), or are you good?

content.text:
I've verified what I can; the rest you must confirm empirically. Here's the honest picture.

## Q1 — are the daemons stopped when logged out? Can't confirm from here.
Verified: both run **now** as GNOME-session autostart apps (`app-gnome-dropbox.scope`, `app-gnome-koofr.scope`, launched by `~/.config/autostart/*.desktop`) — no system service. Normally those stop when GNOME logs out.

**But** you have `Linger=yes`, and I couldn't pin down `KillUserProcesses` on this box (nothing in config → compiled default, which is systemd-version-dependent). With lingering on, and daemons that fork into the background, survival past logout is *possible*. I won't assert either way without proof.

Verify it yourself — this is the one command that settles it. After logging out of GNOME, from an **SSH session or TTY** (the same context you'd run `pc-switcher` from):
```
pgrep -af 'dropbox|storagegui|storagesync'
```
Empty → daemons stopped, safe to touch `~/Dropbox`/`~/Koofr`. Any output → they're still alive; syncing content then = copying a live-written folder (torn) while an active writer races you. Don't.

## Q2 — would syncing content confuse the target daemons?
Not *permanently* — reconciling out-of-band folder changes is normal for them (you edit files with other apps all the time). On next start they rescan, hash, and compare to the cloud. The issue is what a **blind `rsync --delete` mirror** (which is exactly what pc-switcher `folder_sync` does) can do to that reconciliation:

1. **Silent data loss on divergence.** If the travel laptop has any local `~/Dropbox` edit not yet in the cloud, `--delete`/overwrite destroys it, and the daemon then propagates that deletion to the cloud. Letting the daemon sync instead would have preserved both as a *conflicted copy*. So rsync converts a recoverable conflict into silent loss. (Unlikely in your one-machine-at-a-time flow, but it's the sharp edge.)
2. **Selective-sync fights.** You noted selective sync is per-device. If desk syncs a superset, rsyncing desk→travel drops folders travel deliberately excludes; travel's daemon then re-uploads or re-deletes them to honor its own selective-sync → churn.
3. **Internal cache leakage.** `~/Dropbox/.dropbox.cache` **exists** — it must be excluded or you mirror Dropbox's private cache.

**The good case (and your real win):** both daemons fully caught up to the cloud before logout + identical selective sync. Then rsync desk→travel over LAN makes travel's files already-current; travel's daemon rescans, finds local content hash-matches the cloud → no re-upload, no conflict, **no internet re-download.** That's the LAN-speed / cellular-avoidance payoff you're after — legit.

## Recommendation
The benefit is real but conditional; the failure modes (silent loss, selective-sync churn) are nasty and easy to trip. So: **keep it excluded by default.** If you want the LAN pre-seed and will hold the discipline — daemons confirmed stopped, both caught up before logout, identical selective sync, always desk→travel before editing on travel — I can enable it *with guardrails*:
- un-exclude `Dropbox/` and `Koofr/`
- add `/Dropbox/.dropbox.cache` (and any Koofr `.Trash`/metadata) to excludes

Want me to wire up the guardrailed version, or leave the daemons to own their folders?

content: We built a production pc-switcher config.yaml syncing /home/janfr with excludes for caches, secrets, and machine-specific data. It's installed and validated. Next: you decide whether to sync Dropbox/Koofr content over LAN with guardrails, or leave the daemons to handle it. (disable recaps in /config)
